exemplar.title: To what extent did the Libyan crisis affect the European oil economy between 2011 and 2021?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent did the Libyan crisis affect the European oil economy between 2011 and 2021?

strengths: Title and introduction clearly state the topic, time frame (2011â2021), and lens (oil economy).
Research question is explicit, narrow in scope, and consistently repeated.
Methods (historical narrative, trade statistics, price series, policy analysis) are generally appropriate and informed.
weaknesses: Minor ambiguity remains over whether âEuropeâ refers to EU-27 or entire continent.
Methodology is outlined briefly but lacks explicit justification for each data choice.
Concluding sentence is truncated mid-thought, affecting cohesion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strand assessments fell into the 3â4 band: the topic is communicated clearly (but with slight geographic ambiguity), the RQ is clear though slightly drifted in some sections, and methods are appropriate but under-justified.  A holistic 4/6 reflects solid performance within band 3â4.

strengths: Integration of statistical figures (e.g., 1.6 million bpd, export shares) demonstrates effective use of relevant data.
Economic and historical terminology is generally accurate and applied in context.
Factual knowledge of Libyan production and EU import shifts is coherently woven into narrative.
weaknesses: A few secondary web sources (e.g., Brookings blog) are of uneven academic weight.
Formatting glitches (superscript coding) and occasional imprecise phrases reduce polish.
Some assertions on GDP impacts lack rigorous sourcing or quantified verification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All source-selection, knowledge-use and terminology strands sit in band 3â4.  The student shows mostly relevant material and coherent integration, warranting a 4/6 overall.

strengths: Majority of research comes from credible statistical yearbooks and peer-reviewed texts, demonstrating deliberate selection.
Analysis links output declines to price spikes, policy shifts and counter-evidence in a largely coherent manner.
Argument follows a logical interdisciplinary structure, culminating in a well-synthesized conclusion.
weaknesses: Secondary graphics of unclear origin and some generic websites reduce overall rigour.
Critical evaluation (e.g., U.S. shale impact, EU institutional constraints) is present but brief; lacks deeper nuance.
Some data points (e.g., renewable energy share changes) are uncited or would benefit from formal calculations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each critical-thinking strand landed in the 4â6 band (mid-band scores of 5).  Aggregating these and reflecting the essayâs solid but not exceptional analysis, an 8/12 holistically balances the strengths and areas for deeper evaluation.

strengths: Formal title page, table of contents and coherent section headings provide clear academic structure.
In-text citations and bibliography follow a largely consistent system.
Overall formatting and pagination are systematic with only minor inconsistencies.
weaknesses: Several figures are low resolution or screenshots with incomplete captions and missing axis labels.
Footnote URLs are sometimes truncated or broken across lines.
Bibliography entries show inconsistent styling (italics, URL formatting).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Academic structure and referencing merit the top band, but visual-element shortcomings place that strand in band 1â2.  A 3/4 overall reflects mostly clear presentation with notable areas for technical improvement.

strengths: The studentâs chosen topic indicates some personal interest, even if not explicitly reflected.
weaknesses: No RPPF excerpts or reflective commentary included.
No evidence of evaluative reflections on challenges or skill development.
Intellectual initiative and personal engagement are minimal or absent.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three engagement strands fell into the lowest band (1â2), with no substantive reflections or evidence of initiative.  Assigning 1/6 reflects this minimal engagement.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay follows a logical progression (pre-war dependency â immediate impacts â long-term adaptations â conclusion) and blends economics and history, showing interdisciplinary intent.  Critical insight appears in discussions of rentier-state vulnerabilities, yet evaluation of alternative perspectives is limited; thus the argument is structured but not nuanced, situating it in the 4-6 band (score 5).

In-text citations and a bibliography follow a consistent superscript/footnote system; most entries are complete and alphabetised.  A few malformed URLs and duplicated author names are present, but overall formatting is largely correct, meriting 3 marks in the highest band.

The student states the topic in both the title and introductory paragraph and consistently returns to it in each major section.  The geographical area (Europe), the time frame (2011-2021) and the thematic lens (oil economy) are all explicit, so the reader can readily grasp the focus.  Minor ambiguity persists over whether the study treats âEuropeâ as the EU-27 or a wider region, but overall the communication is mostly clear, justifying placement in the 3-4 band rather than the top band.

A formal title page, table of contents, section headings and bibliography are provided, and the internal structure is coherent.  Minor pagination/formatting inconsistencies exist, so a lower score within the top band (3 of 4) is justified.

The student demonstrates sound factual knowledge of Libyan production data, EU import shares and post-war policy shifts, weaving figures into the narrative.  Integration is adequate, yet some assertions on GDP impacts or inflation lack rigorous sourcing or quantified verification, so coherence is good but not âexpertly integrated.â
No RPPF excerpts are provided, so there is no evidence of evaluative reflection.  The minimal descriptor therefore applies (1-2 band, bottom mark).

The student links falling Libyan output to Brent price spikes, GDP revisions and policy shifts, supporting most claims with data.  Evaluation of counter-evidence (e.g., the role of U.S. shale in dampening prices) is present but brief, so conclusions are âlargely supportedâ but not incisively critiqued, warranting upper-mid band marks.

The research question is explicitly worded, narrow in scope (one conflict, one commodity, a defined decade) and repeated verbatim.  Nevertheless, later sections drift slightly into broader EU renewable policy and geopolitical rivalries, so the focus is only "mostly" rather than "fully" maintained.  This matches the descriptor of a clear but only partially focused RQ.

Most research is drawn from credible statistical yearbooks and peer-reviewed texts, demonstrating deliberate selection.  Some secondary graphics of unclear origin and a handful of generic websites reduce overall rigour, so a mid-band (4-6) score of 5 is appropriate rather than the highest band.

There is scant evidence of intellectual initiative beyond standard secondary research; primary data collection or novel methodological choices are absent.  Consequently, the strand fits the lowest descriptive band with 1 mark.

The essay employs historical narrative, trade-flow statistics, price series and policy analysisâan appropriate interdisciplinary mixâyet the method is described only briefly in the introduction with little explicit justification of why each data set or discipline was chosen.  Consequently the selection appears informed but not âfullyâ justified, meriting the 3-4 band.

Roughly 70 scholarly, governmental and industry sources are cited, most directly relevant (OPEC, Eurostat, IEA, IMF, peer-reviewed histories).  A few secondary websites (Brookings article, ResearchGate images) are of uneven academic weight, so the selection is âmostlyâ but not âconsistentlyâ relevant, fitting the 3-4 range.

Although the essay topic appears personally chosen, no explicit commentary on the studentâs motivation or learning journey is supplied.  Only minimal personal engagement can be inferred, justifying a score of 1 in the 1-2 band.

Several figures are included, but some are screenshots with low resolution, incomplete captions or missing source details.  Placement occasionally interrupts text flow.  This meets basic requirements but lacks full academic quality, matching the 1-2 band (score 2).

Economic and historical terminologyâe.g., ârentier state,â âstrategic petroleum reserves,â âdiversificationââis generally accurate and used in context.  Occasional formatting glitches (e.g., coding of superscripts) and a few imprecise phrases prevent a fully polished application, placing it solidly in the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The topic is clearly stated, but specifying whether âEuropeâ refers to the EU-27 or the entire continent would enhance precision and eliminate interpretive ambiguity.
The inclusion of a word count at the beginning meets the formal requirement, demonstrating attention to formatting expectations.
The title concisely communicates the research focus on Libyaâs conflict and Europeâs oil economy, immediately orienting the reader to the studyâs scope.
The methodology is outlined briefly but lacks explicit justification for each chosen data type (e.g., why trade-flow or price series). Adding rationale would strengthen methodological clarity.
The introduction integrates statistical figures (e.g., 1.6 million bpd, export shares) effectively, demonstrating solid use of relevant source data to establish context.
Footnote formatting appears inconsistent (mixed sup tags and OCR artifacts). Standardizing superscript citation markup will improve readability.
The table lacks a clear unit annotation or explanatory note for âbarrels per day,â which may confuse readers. Adding a unit label above the columns would clarify interpretation.
Caption simply reads âFigure 1â without a descriptive title. Including a concise caption (e.g., âLibyan oil exports by region, 2004â2008â) would improve comprehension.
Figure 2 appears as a low-resolution screenshot, and the source is not clearly cited. Ensure higher-quality visuals and include complete source details below the image.
Inline math markup (e.g., 2013<sup>34</sup>) disrupts narrative flow. Use proper citation formatting rather than embedding raw tags in the main text.
The mention of U.S. shale dampening oil prices is brief. A deeper critical evaluation, perhaps contrasting supply elasticity across regions, would sharpen analysis.
Analysis of EU policy shifts is coherent but lacks assessment of coordination challenges across member states. Critically discussing institutional constraints would deepen evaluation.
Several footnote URLs in the middle of the text are truncated or broken across lines. Review and correct URLs for completeness and consistency.
Figure 4 lacks clearly labeled axes and a source citation beneath it. Adding axis titles and a âSource:â line will meet academic visual conventions.
Renewable energy share figures are compelling, but the paragraph lacks an explicit citation for the 12.5% â 22% change. Adding a parenthetical or superscript will strengthen credibility.
The Brookings Institution source is a policy blog rather than peer-reviewed research. Consider substituting with an academic journal or official report for stronger scholarly weight.
Inline math tags (e.g., 0.6â0.9\%) appear in the running text. Replace raw LaTeX or math tags with formatted expressions such as $$0.6\%-0.9\%$$.
The concluding sentence on page 22 is truncated mid-thought. Completing the final paragraph will ensure the reader sees a cohesive wrap-up to the research question.
The conclusion effectively synthesizes interdisciplinary insights linking the oil shock to long-term policy change, demonstrating coherent argument structure.
Bibliography entries exhibit inconsistent stylingâsome titles italicized, others not, and URLs variably formatted. Standardize reference style throughout.
The section on Libyaâs pre-2011 instability would benefit from linking economic vulnerability theories (e.g., supply shock elasticity) to the narrative, deepening conceptual integration.
The rapid release of strategic reserves is described clearly, but integrating a quantitative priceâimpact formula (e.g., $$\Delta P/P$$) could provide rigorous analytical depth.
Section headings lack a uniform numbering style (e.g., âSection 3:â vs. plain âConclusionâ). Consistent heading formatting will improve academic structure.
In the price-impact paragraph, consider presenting the calculation formally with $$\frac{120-90}{90}\times100\%=33\%$$ to illustrate the magnitude of the spike.
